data.title: Is It Time To Reassess Kenvue (KVUE) After A Mixed Year Of Share Price Moves
data.description:
• If you are looking at Kenvue and wondering whether the current share price reflects the true value of the business, you are not alone. This article is built to help you frame that question clearly.
  • Over the past week the stock has returned a 3.8% decline, compared with a 2.1% gain over the last month, a 6.2% return year to date, and an 18.1% decline over the past year. This gives you a mixed starting point for thinking about future risk and reward.
  • This valuation check is being published as part of ongoing evergreen coverage to give investors a consistent reference point, rather than as a reaction to a single event or short term headline. It can be a useful companion to any company specific news you are already following, such as product launches, legal updates, portfolio changes or management moves you might have seen elsewhere.
  • Kenvue currently scores 2 out of 6 on our valuation checks, as shown in our valuation summary. Next we will walk through the main valuation approaches behind that score and then finish with a way to put those numbers into a broader context.

Kenvue scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Kenvue Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow model takes estimates of a company’s future cash flows and discounts them back to today using a required return, to arrive at an estimate of what the whole business might be worth in today’s dollars.

For Kenvue, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $1.74b. Analysts have provided explicit forecasts for several years, and Simply Wall St extends these out so that free cash flow reaches a projected $2.74b in 2030, with intermediate years such as 2026 to 2029 stepping up between those points.

When those projected cash flows, including the ten year span from 2026 to 2035, are discounted back to today, the DCF output suggests an intrinsic value of $29.67 per share. Compared with the current share price, this implies a 38.0% discount, which points to Kenvue trading below this cash flow based estimate of value.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) analysis suggests Kenvue is undervalued by 38.0%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 50 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Kenvue.

Approach 2: Kenvue Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Kenvue, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you pay today directly to the earnings the company is already generating. It is a simple shortcut for asking how many dollars investors are currently willing to pay for each dollar of earnings.

What counts as a “normal” P/E depends on how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk. Higher growth and lower perceived risk can support a higher multiple, while slower growth or higher risk usually points to a lower, more cautious multiple.

Kenvue currently trades on a P/E of 23.98x. That sits above the Personal Products industry average of 21.16x and above the peer group average of 20.75x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary Fair Ratio of 22.88x, which reflects factors such as Kenvue’s earnings profile, profit margins, industry, market size and specific risks.

This Fair Ratio goes a step further than basic peer or industry comparisons because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all peers deserve the same multiple. With Kenvue’s actual P/E sitting modestly above the Fair Ratio, this framework points to the shares trading at a somewhat richer level than that tailored benchmark.

Result: OVERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your Kenvue Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, a simple way for you to put a clear story behind the numbers by linking your view of Kenvue’s future revenue, earnings and margins to a financial forecast, a fair value, and then a comparison with the current share price. All of this is available within an easy tool on Simply Wall St’s Community page that updates automatically as fresh news or earnings arrive and can reflect very different views. For example, one investor may set a fair value near US$25.09 based on higher growth and margins, while another may anchor closer to about US$19.67 using more cautious assumptions. This helps you quickly see how your own Kenvue Narrative translates into decisions about whether the current price looks high, low, or roughly in line with what you think the business is worth.

data.title: Piaggio And C (BIT:PIA) Margin Compression To 2.4% Tests Bullish Earnings Narrative
data.description:
Piaggio & C (BIT:PIA) has laid out a steady set of FY 2025 numbers, with third quarter revenue of €351.8 million and basic EPS of €0.004. Earlier quarters saw revenue at €481.9 million with EPS of €0.061 in Q2, and €370.7 million with EPS of €0.025 in Q1. Over the trailing twelve months to the third quarter of 2025, revenue came in at about €1.5 billion with basic EPS of €0.104. This frames the latest quarter within a year of modest profitability, where net profit margins sit at 2.4% compared with 3.9% a year earlier.

See our full analysis for Piaggio & C.

With the headline figures on the table, the next step is to set these results against the dominant stories around Piaggio & C to see which narratives are supported by the numbers and which start to look less convincing.

See what the community is saying about Piaggio & C

Advertisement

Margins Slip To 2.4% On Lower Profit

  • Over the last twelve months, Piaggio & C generated about €1.5b of revenue and €36.7 million of net income, which equates to a 2.4% net margin compared with 3.9% a year earlier.
  • What stands out for the bearish narrative is that this thinner 2.4% margin sits alongside a modest 3.3% average yearly earnings growth over five years. Critics link this to pressures such as high logistics costs and tougher competition, even though recent quarters like Q2 FY 2025 still showed net income of €21.4 million on €481.9 million of revenue.
    • Bears argue that the drop from 3.9% to 2.4% margin fits with concerns about rising costs and market share pressure in core regions.
    • At the same time, the fact that Piaggio & C remained profitable every quarter in the period, with net income between about €1.5 million and €33.4 million, shows earnings are under pressure but not collapsing, which challenges the harshest bearish claims.
If you want to see how skeptics connect these margin figures to their long term worries about demand and electrification, have a look at 🐻 Piaggio & C Bear Case

Earnings Forecasts Outrun 2.4% Revenue Growth

  • Forecasts point to earnings growing about 21% per year over the next three years while revenue is expected to grow about 2.4% per year from the current trailing level of roughly €1.5b.
  • Supporters of the bullish view see this gap between faster earnings growth and slower top line growth as a sign that margin expansion could become a key earnings driver. This lines up with expectations that profit margins rise from about 2.9% to 6.4% and that earnings reach €123.6 million by around 2028.
    • Backers of this bullish case point to the recent record gross margin and EBITDA mentioned in management commentary, arguing that the current 2.4% net margin does not fully reflect operational improvements already in place.
    • However, the fact that trailing net income of €36.7 million is well below the bullish €123.6 million earnings goal underlines how much improvement is being assumed compared with what the recent numbers show.
If you are curious how these growth assumptions stack up against the recent €36.7 million in earnings, you can read the bullish story in full at 🐂 Piaggio & C Bull Case

P/E Of 16x Versus €1.37 DCF Fair Value

  • With the share price at about €1.67, Piaggio & C trades on a trailing P/E close to 16x, a little below peers on 17.9x and the global auto group on 18.7x, while a DCF fair value of €1.37 sits below the current price.
  • Consensus narrative highlights this as a mixed setup where relatively low earnings multiples and a 4.8% dividend yield look appealing, yet weaker free cash flow coverage of that dividend and interest payments that are not well covered by earnings are important checks on the story.
    • The P/E discount versus peers supports the idea that the market is not paying a premium despite the forecasts for faster earnings growth.
    • At the same time, the gap between the €1.67 share price and the €1.37 DCF fair value, together with the flagged interest coverage and cash flow stresses, provides concrete reasons why some investors may treat the lower multiple as compensation for these risks rather than a clear bargain.

For someone considering Federal Home Loan Mortgage, the big-picture belief is that a large, systemically important mortgage guarantor can still create value even while it is unprofitable and under conservatorship, provided it maintains relevance in the US housing finance system and manages its risks. Recent results show net income easing from 2024 to 2025 and basic losses per share, so near term, investors are watching credit quality, capital requirements and any policy shifts around the GSE framework more than headline revenue growth. Approach 1: Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much value a bank creates over and above the return that shareholders require. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it starts from the equity on the balance sheet and asks whether the forecast return on that equity justifies the current share price.

For Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena, book value is €19.98 per share and the stable book value used in the model is €8.99 per share, based on future book value estimates from 6 analysts. Stable EPS is €0.88 per share, sourced from weighted future return on equity estimates from 10 analysts, while the average return on equity used is 9.78%.

The model applies a cost of equity of €0.90 per share. For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful, quick way to relate what you pay for a share to the earnings that each share generates. It helps you see how many years of current earnings the market is effectively pricing in.

What counts as a “normal” or “fair” P/E depends on what investors expect for future growth and how risky they see those earnings. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk often go with a higher P/E, while lower expected growth or higher perceived risk often goes with a lower P/E.

Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena currently trades on a P/E of 8.02x. That compares with an Italian Banks industry average of about 10.87x and a peer group average of 12.19x, so the stock is on a lower multiple than both of these simple benchmarks.

data.title: Is Walt Disney (DIS) Fairly Priced After Recent Share Price Weakness And Media Sector Reassessment
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether Walt Disney shares still justify their price, this article walks through what the current market value might be implying about the business.
  • The stock last closed at US$101.54, and recent returns show a 4.2% fall over the past week, a 5.1% decline over the last month, and a 9.2% decline year to date, with a 2.7% decline over the past year and an 11.1% gain over three years, while the five year return sits at a 47.3% decline.
  • Recent news coverage has focused on how investors are reassessing large media and entertainment groups as they balance traditional TV, parks, and streaming. For Disney, that conversation has kept attention on how its mix of assets is being priced, which helps frame why the share price has moved in the way it has.
  • On Simply Wall St's 6 point valuation checklist, Walt Disney scores a 4 out of 6 for potential undervaluation. Next, we will look at how different valuation methods arrive at that view, and then finish with a way to tie them together into an even clearer picture of value.

Find out why Walt Disney's -2.7% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Walt Disney Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model takes estimates of future cash a business could generate and discounts those back into today’s dollars to arrive at an implied value per share.

For Walt Disney, the model uses last twelve months Free Cash Flow of about $8.65b as a starting point, then projects future Free Cash Flow in dollars using analyst estimates for the next few years and extrapolated numbers after that. For example, Simply Wall St’s projections include Free Cash Flow of $10.18b in 2026 and $11.24b in 2028, extending out to $15.06b by 2035.

Discounting this stream of projected cash flows back to today using a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity model gives an estimated intrinsic value of about $95.72 per share. Compared with the recent share price of $101.54, this DCF output suggests the stock is around 6.1% overvalued, which is a relatively small gap and within a range many investors might see as broadly in line with fundamentals.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Walt Disney is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Walt Disney.

Approach 2: Walt Disney Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like Walt Disney, the P/E ratio is a straightforward way to connect what you pay for each share with the earnings that support that price. Investors usually look for a P/E that lines up with their expectations for future growth and the level of risk they are taking on, with higher growth or lower perceived risk often justifying a higher "normal" P/E ratio.

Disney currently trades on a P/E of about 14.7x. That sits below the Entertainment industry average of around 35.2x and also below the peer group average of about 48.0x. On the surface, that gap indicates the market is valuing Disney’s earnings at a lower multiple than many other companies in its space.

Simply Wall St’s Fair Ratio for Disney is 25.3x. This is a proprietary estimate of the P/E that might be expected given Disney’s earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and risk factors. Because it adjusts for these company specific traits, the Fair Ratio can provide a more tailored reference point than a simple comparison with broad industry or peer averages. Compared with the current P/E of 14.7x, the Fair Ratio shows the shares are trading below that modeled level.

Result: UNDERVALUED

A Narrative is your own thesis written out in plain language, then linked directly to a financial forecast and a fair value estimate. Instead of just seeing that one investor thinks Disney is worth about US$95.94 per share and another lands closer to US$134.63 or about US$131.50, you can see the reasoning behind those views and decide which story feels closer to your own.
